text:
the bitcoin community has argued about quantum security for years and landed nowhere

the reason is simple 

every solution on the table asks bitcoin to change something it was built never to change

→ a hard fork splits the network
→ freezing exposed coins breaks the credibility of self-custody
→ migrating funds to new address types breaks the wallets people already trust

the conservative side of crypto rejected all three, and they were right to

@quipnetwork looked at the same problem and approached it from a completely different direction

instead of changing bitcoin, they built a layer that wraps around it

→ post-quantum protection sits on top of the existing chain
→ your coins never move
→ no fork, no migration, no new address scheme
→ self-custody stays exactly where it is

the protection is real and already running, not a proposal waiting on a vote

→ 20k+ wallets already covered
→ 500+ nodes operating
→ 13k+ active users on the network

quantum breaking bitcoin is a question of when, not if

@quipnetwork is one of the only teams that built the answer before the deadline forced everyone to care

$QUIP is the asset attached to that layer

text:
the next leap in ai isn't bigger models, it's better problems to solve

the bottleneck shifted a while ago and most people are still focused on parameter counts instead of compute architecture

@quipnetwork sits at the part of the stack everyone is going to need next

why ai training hits a wall

training large models means solving optimization problems with billions of parameters

classical computers do this by brute force, billions of small calculations running in parallel for weeks at a time

at some point the energy and time cost stops being worth the marginal gain

quantum hardware approaches the same problems differently, finding optimal configurations across massive search spaces in a fraction of the steps

$QUIP turns that capability into a marketplace anyone can tap into

the robotics problem nobody talks about

robotics needs something ai labs don't, real time decisions in physical environments

path planning, sensor fusion, motion control, grasp optimization, all of it requires solving combinatorial problems on the fly

classical chips inside robots can handle simple cases but break down once the environment gets complex enough

@quipnetwork lets a robot offload those calculations to a quantum compute layer in seconds

the result is robots that can react to messy real world conditions instead of memorized scenarios

what makes the network design different

most quantum compute access today goes through cloud providers with api keys, fiat billing, and waiting queues

none of that scales for ai agents or autonomous robots that need to act independently

$QUIP gives the same access programmatically, no human in the middle, no billing infrastructure to fight with

compute jobs route through a token, not a contract negotiation

why the timing actually matters

ai labs are running into compute walls right now and robotics companies are quietly building toward physical deployment

both need a new layer of compute to keep moving forward

@quipnetwork is the only project building that layer with crypto rails underneath

q2 2026 mainnet timing puts this directly in the window where ai compute scarcity becomes a real bottleneck

the people positioning now are the ones who saw the convergence coming before the rest of the market did

text:
🚨 YOUR BIGGEST TRADING BOT MISTAKE

You ran your strategy through Claude.

Got 74% win rate.
Looked incredible.

Deployed live and it started bleeding from day one.

This is what happens when you test with the wrong tool.

Here is exactly why AI backtesting is worthless and what actually works:

When you ask an AI to backtest a Polymarket strategy it does one thing.

Compares the price at your entry point to the final resolution price.

If your entry was below resolution, it calls it a win.

That is the entire model.

No order book depth.

No fill probability at your entry price.

No other bots competing for the same position simultaneously.

No latency between your signal firing and your order actually reaching the book.

No adverse selection (the uncomfortable reality that when you bid at 40 cents you disproportionately get filled when the market is about to move against you).

No slippage from thin liquidity at the exact moment you need to enter.

The AI looks at a start price and an end price and draws a straight line between them.

Real markets do not have straight lines.

They have order books with depth that changes every millisecond.

They have other bots that take fills before you.

They have latency that means you never actually get the price you saw when your signal fired.

Real back test solves all of this.

Sub-second historical data with full order book depth at every tick.

You are not simulating against closing prices.

You are simulating against what the actual book looked like at the exact moment your bot would have fired.

Fill probability is modeled from real book depth.

Slippage is calculated from actual liquidity at your entry level.

Latency is built into the simulation so your modeled execution reflects what real execution looks like.

The difference in results is brutal.

A strategy showing 74% win rate in AI backtesting typically comes out at 52-56%.

That is still tradeable.

But you need to know the real number before you deploy capital on the fake one.

My bot had negative PnL before I tested it right.

Now it's sitting on $127k PnL.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/UoAjMxAPUI>

Test with real data or do not test at all, hope you get my point.

text:
🚨 THIS TIP WILL MAKE YOUR BOT PRINT PASSIVELY

Most builders obsess over finding the perfect signal.

Meanwhile their entry price is quietly destroying every edge they think they have.

I spent months recording crypto tick data on Polymarket and what i found changed how i build everything.

Thanks to this analysis, my bot is finally sitting at 6 figs profit.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/UoAjMxAPUI>

Price behavior at specific levels is remarkably consistent.

Not because of market structure or technical analysis.

Because humans panic and FOMO at the same price points every single time regardless of which asset they are trading.

Here is what the data actually shows:

The closer you are to 70-75 cents on entry, the higher the probability of seeing a significant drawdown before resolution.

We are talking double digit drops happening more than half the time at those levels.

Most bots enter at whatever price the signal fires and set a fixed SL percentage based on nothing.

The ones that survive long term are built around what historically happens at each specific price point.

Not guesses.
Not rounded numbers.

Actual statistical behavior from thousands of real market observations.

This changes three things in your bot immediately:

Where you place stop losses - based on historical drawdown depth at your entry level not arbitrary percentages.

When you hedge - data tells you which price points have the highest probability of a significant move against you before resolution.

How you size positions - entries at lower price points carry different risk profiles than higher ones and your sizing should reflect that.

I share all of this here for free because most people building bots are flying blind.

Will drop another useful post tomorrow.

Peace.

Belt and suspenders.
Never trusting one layer of protection again.

If that triggers the bot pauses automatically and sends me an alert.

Just woke up.

My bot already printed +$4,800 while I was asleep.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/9ouws5ZyU1>

Most bots bleed because of those 3 small mistakes:

Your win rate looks fine but PnL keeps going negative.

This specific combination means one thing almost every time.

You are entering at the wrong price relative to your win rate.

And no amount of signal improvement will fix it.

Here is the math nobody explains clearly:

If your strategy wins 65% of the time but you are entering at 70 cents - you are losing money.

You need to win more than 70% just to break even at that entry price.

Win rate and entry price are inseparable.
You cannot optimize one without the other.

The fix is not finding a higher win rate signal.

The fix is finding the upstream reason why high win rate entries appear at lower prices.

Entry price itself is almost always selection bias masking the real signal underneath.

Dig one layer deeper and find what actually creates that favorable entry.

Second problem that kills bots slowly.

Asymmetric scalps at 85 cents and above.

Feels safe because you win constantly.

One reversal in the final 30 seconds erases days of accumulated profit instantly.

Third problem most people ignore until it is too late.

Betting full size on single entries.

Split every position into 3 to 4 chunks instead.

Enter the first chunk.
If price moves in your favor add more.

If it moves against you immediately - your stop loss only hits on a fraction of intended size.

Kelly sizing on top of chunked entries dramatically reduces drawdown during losing clusters without touching your edge on winning runs.

Simple execution discipline beats complex signals every single time.

Full guide will be quoted below this post.

One article with my public results will fix your bot quick.

text:
🚨 FOR POLYMARKET DEVS AND BOT OWNERS:

Your bot is sizing positions wrong and you have no idea.

No error message.
No obvious failure.
Just quietly worse fills and confused risk management.

This is one of the most overlooked mistakes in Polymarket bot building.

Here is exactly what is happening and why it matters:

Most people think about position sizing in USD.

I want to risk $50 on this trade.
My bot calculates the order in dollar terms.

Simple, right? Nope.

Polymarket does not work in USD.

It works in shares.

And the math between those two things is not as straightforward as it looks.

When you calculate in USD first and convert to shares second - rounding errors accumulate.

Small discrepancies between what your bot thinks it ordered and what actually hit the book.

At low prices like 3 cents or 4 cents those rounding errors become significant as a percentage of your position.

A bot ordering at 3 cents thinking it placed a $30 position might actually have placed $28.47 worth.

That gap matters for two reasons.

First - your actual risk exposure differs from what your risk management system thinks it is.

Your stop losses and position limits are calculating against wrong numbers.

Second - across thousands of trades daily those small discrepancies compound into real PnL drag that never shows up as a single obvious loss.

It just slowly makes everything slightly worse than it should be.

The fix is to flip the calculation order completely.

My bot had negative PnL before I fixed those issues.

Now i'm sitting at $100,000 PnL.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/9ouws5ZyU1>

Decide on shares first.
Then derive the USD cost from the share count and current price.

Never go USD to shares.
Always go shares to USD.

This keeps your order quantities precise and your risk management system working against accurate numbers.

It sounds like a minor implementation detail.

Across 5,000 trades per day it is the difference between clean execution and a bot that always underperforms its own backtest without any obvious reason why.

Hope this will help you.

I also recommend you to go through the whole article where I shared the most common mistakes and my public 6 figs results.

Leaving it quoted below.

text:
🚨 THIS MISTAKE KILLS YOUR BOT:

Every bot builder skips straight to live trading.

Every bot builder learns the same expensive lesson afterward.

There are three stages between having a strategy and having a profitable bot.

Most people only use one of them and wonder why everything falls apart immediately.

Here is exactly what each stage does and why skipping any of them is guaranteed to cost you real money:

Paper mode is the first stage.

Your bot runs completely simulated trades against real market data.

No wallet.
No orders.
No real fills.

Just your logic processing live prices and logging what it would have done.

This stage tells you whether your strategy logic is actually working the way you think it is.

Most strategies have at least 5 bugs in the logic that only appear when running against live data.

Paper mode finds them for free.

If not this, I'd never hit $100k PnL on my best case bot so far.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/9ouws5ZyU1>

Shadow mode is the second stage and the one almost everyone skips.

Your bot connects to real infrastructure with a real wallet but submits zero-balance orders.

It attempts actual API calls.
Processes real rejections.
Handles real latency.

Everything is live except capital is at risk.

This is where ghost fill handling breaks.

Where nonce management fails under real conditions.

Where your reconciliation loop proves whether it actually works or just looks right in code.

Every NSF rejection.
Every timeout.
Every unexpected API response.

These are signals your backtest never saw and shadow mode surfaces them before they cost you anything.

Live mode is the final stage.

Small size first.
Not full deployment immediately.

Start with 10% of intended capital and verify that live results match shadow mode results within acceptable variance.

Only then scale.

The reason most bots die in the first week is not bad strategy.

It is deploying full capital into infrastructure that was never stress-tested against real market conditions.

Paper mode finds logic bugs.

Shadow mode finds execution bugs.

Skipping both means live mode finds everything at full cost.

Read my full guide (quoted below) if you wanna dive deeper into the most common mistakes.

First order placed gets filled.
Second place gets nothing.

A bot 100ms slower than a colocated Asian market maker is not competing.

The competitive reality post-KYC colocation is that the most profitable edges in short-window crypto markets are now dominated by foreign professional setups.

Ireland and Dublin VPS setups are currently the closest viable option for non-Asian developers.

Not perfect.
But dramatically better than US domestic.

The practical advice is blunt.

If your 5-minute BTC bot was profitable before V2 and is now bleeding, geographic latency combined with increased professional competition is likely the reason.

The game changed.

text:
🚨 STOP USELESS AI BACKTESTING

Your backtest showed 74% win rate.

Live trading is bleeding from day one.

This is not bad luck.
This is a fundamental problem with how you tested.

And AI made it worse by making the lie look convincing.

Here is exactly what is broken and how to actually fix it:

When you ask Claude or ChatGPT to backtest a Polymarket strategy it looks at one thing.

Strike price versus closing price.

That is it.

It has no idea what the order book looked like at your entry moment.

It does not know how many other bots were competing for the same fill.

It cannot model the 40ms latency between your signal and your actual execution.

It ignores adverse selection:

The uncomfortable reality that when you bid 10 cents below ask at 40 cents you disproportionately get filled on the losing side because smarter money is taking the other side of your order.

So your AI backtest produces a beautiful equity curve based on a world that does not exist.

You deploy into a world that does.

Here is what accurate backtesting actually requires:

Record your own tick data.
Every market.
Every timeframe.

Not public data.
Your own recordings from your own websocket setup.

Full order book depth at every tick.
Best bid and ask.
Fill rates at each price level.
Your actual latency recorded in real time.

Without this you are modeling a fantasy.

Once you have real data the backtesting workflow changes completely.

Simulate slippage from actual book depth not theoretical midpoint fills.

Model fill probability at each price level based on what the book actually showed.

Include your real latency in every simulated entry.

Run calibration constantly against live results until your backtest matches live performance within 3%.

Not 10%.
Within 3%, you heard me right.

Anything wider means your model has a blind spot that will cost you real money.

The painful truth is that most strategies showing 70%+ win rate in AI backtests land at 52-56% in real markets.

That is still usable.

text:
Your bot is losing because of dirty data.
Not bad strategy.

Most developers spend weeks perfecting signal logic and never touch their websocket setup.

That is the single most expensive mistake in Polymarket bot building.

Here is exactly what is happening and how to fix it completely:

Raw Polymarket websockets are full of stale snapshots, missed ticks, jitter and brief disconnects.

Your bot is reacting to prices that do not exist in the real order book.

That is not a strategy problem.
That is an infrastructure problem.

Here is the full fix system layer by layer:

Start every connection 15 seconds before the window opens.

In the final 5 seconds before trading begins - require at least 3 ticks per token with no single price jump above 5 cents.

If your connection fails that test - skip the entire window completely.

Trading on a bad connection is worse than not trading at all.

Never run one websocket.
Run 100 to 300 parallel connections per feed.

Every 4 seconds kill the slowest 10% and respawn them.

Your bot always takes the first deduplicated tick from whichever socket wins the race.

More connections means higher probability of getting clean data before competitors.

Compare every incoming tick against the last known price from your warmup period.

Any tick with a delta above 15 cents gets rejected immediately and logged.

Stale data entering your decision logic is how a working strategy becomes a bleeding one.

Drop the very first tick from every new connection without exception.

It is almost always Polymarket's cached orderbook snapshot from before the window opened.

Acting on it puts you in trades based on prices that are already gone.

Spread your connection startups evenly across one full second.

Never launch all sockets simultaneously.

Staggered startup gives each connection a better shot at receiving genuinely different market data.

Track timing variance per connection using a jitter EMA.

Cull the most erratic connections first.

Give new sockets 8 seconds to stabilize before including them in the cull cycle.

The result of running all six layers together is dramatically cleaner data than 99% of competing bots ever see.

My best bot was also losing before.

Now it's sitting at $100k PnL.

Public wallet: <https://t.co/UoAjMxAPUI>

Clean data alone has turned losing strategies into profitable ones without changing a single line of trading logic.

Silent fill bug is quietly destroying more Polymarket bots than any bad strategy ever will.

Here is exactly what happens and how to fix it permanently:

You fire an order.

API times out or returns an unclear response.

Your bot assumes the order failed and moves on.

Meanwhile the order actually filled on-chain.

Now your bot has no idea it is holding a position.

It fires again on the next signal.

Suddenly you are doubled up on a trade you never intended to take.

Or worse - you are trying to hedge something your bot does not know exists.

State management becomes completely unreliable.

PnL tracking breaks silently.

Risk controls stop working because they are based on positions your bot thinks are empty.

This gets dramatically worse post-V2 because latency is higher and more variable than before.

Timeouts happen more frequently. API responses are less predictable.

The fix is straightforward but most people never implement it.

Never trust only the API response to confirm fill status.

After any timeout or unclear response - immediately poll the order status endpoint directly.

Verify the actual on-chain state before your bot takes any next action.

On top of that run a full reconciliation pass every 30 seconds as a background process.

Compare what your bot thinks it holds against what is actually sitting on-chain.

Any mismatch gets flagged and corrected immediately before it compounds.

One reconciliation loop prevents the entire category of silent fill problems.

Most bots that mysteriously start losing after working fine are running without this check.

Add it before anything else.

text:
🚨 THIS WILL FIX YOUR TRADING BOT:

Your bot found the perfect entry.
No liquidity.
Trade dead before it starts.

This kills more bot strategies than bad signals ever will.

I ran into this wall dozens of times building bots on Polymarket.

Here is the solution most devs never figure out and it changed everything for me.

Two ways to manufacture your own liquidity from scratch:

Method 1 - Naked sells.

You do not need to own a share to sell it.

Most of the time liquidity is paired - buy shares and sell shares are the same thing from opposite sides.

Buying one side is literally the same as selling the other.

Use this when the side you want has no depth but the opposite side does.

Method 2 - Split shares.

This one is more powerful.

You can split $1 USDC from your wallet into 1 UP share and 1 DOWN share simultaneously.

The split shares themselves have no standalone value until resolution.

If you split and do nothing - one side wins at resolution and you get your $1 back.

Here is how you use this to manufacture entries:

You want to buy a share at 60 cents but there is no liquidity available.

When the window opened you pre-split 10 shares as preparation.

They are now sitting in your wallet.

You list the DOWN shares for sale at 40 cents.

If they fill, you are left holding the UP share you wanted.

Effective entry: 60 cents.
Manufactured from nothing.

This works because the split mechanic creates shares independently of what the order book is showing.

No liquidity in the market does not mean no position available.

It means you need to create it yourself before the window gets crowded.

Combined with the regime filtering and latency optimizations i broke down in my $100k month bot guide.

This solves the last major execution gap that kills profitable strategies before they even get a real test.

Most bots die from conditions they never modeled.
Liquidity gaps are one of the most common and most fixable.

text:
Ghost fills post got a lot of attention from devs.

Someone reached out with a concrete proposal to fix the resolution manipulation problem specifically in 5 and 15 minute BTC markets.

Worth sharing because this is exactly the kind of thinking Polymarket needs right now.

The core problem with current resolution:

One aggressive price move in the final second can flip the outcome entirely.

Single tick manipulation costs almost nothing to execute and completely changes who wins.

The proposed fix is called a Fair close price model.

Instead of resolving on one final price tick, the market uses the last 20 seconds of price data.

Sample BTC index price once per second across that window.

Calculate the median of all 20 samples.

Filter out any ticks that deviate more than 5-10 basis points from that median.

Average the remaining valid samples.

That average becomes the resolution price.

Why this works better than the current system:

Right now manipulating resolution costs one aggressive order in one second.

Under this model an attacker needs to sustain manipulated pricing across most of a 20-second window.

The cost and risk of manipulation increases dramatically.

A single wick in the final second gets filtered out completely.

There is also a recency-weighted version where samples closer to expiry carry more weight.

This keeps the resolution behaving like a true market close while removing obvious microstructure attacks.

The window is short enough that it does not drift from the real terminal price.

It just smooths against manipulation.

This would not fix ghost fills directly.

But combined with a settlement timing fix it would make 5 and 15 minute markets significantly harder to game.

Sharing because the community should push for this.

@Polymarket this is worth serious consideration.

text:
🚨: FOR DEVS AND TRADING BOT OWNERS

Polymarket migrated to a new version.

But ghost fills are still happening on day one of Polymarket V2.

The upgrade everyone was waiting for dropped.
But the most frustrating bug survived it.

Here's why this still matters for every active trader:

CLOB V2 removed the easy cancellation path through incrementNonce.

That was supposed to close the ghost fill exploit.

But the fundamental problem was never just the nonce mechanic.

The real issue is the time gap between off-chain order matching and on-chain settlement.

That gap still exists in V2.

Which means attackers can still match your order off-chain and cancel before it settles on-chain.

Your trade shows as filled. Nothing actually happened. Your position is empty.

Polymarket's new VP of Engineering William Legate posted a response today acknowledging it directly:

"Cancellations are one of the most frustrating issues we're facing right now and we'll be rolling out a full fix very soon."

That acknowledgment is good.
Devs know it exists and they are working on it.

But market makers and bot builders have heard "very soon" before.

Ghost fills have been public knowledge for months.

They survived a major infrastructure overhaul.

The nonce path is closed but the settlement gap is still wide open.

Until the off-chain to on-chain timing is fixed at the architecture level - this attack remains viable.

For anyone running high frequency strategies or LP positions right now:

Size down on anything where a ghost fill significantly hurts your position.

Monitor fills carefully and verify on-chain before assuming execution was clean.

@Polymarket we need this to be fixed ASAP.

My bot is stuck and can't break through $100k PnL for days already.

An average human is about 1.8 meters (6 feet) tall. 

Standing next to Starship V3 would make you look almost microscopic. 

At 124.4 meters (408 feet) tall when fully stacked, Starship V3 is the tallest and most powerful rocket ever built, standing about 69 times taller than an average person. 

It is also around 1.3 meters (4 feet) taller than the previous V2 version, giving SpaceX even more performance for future missions to the Moon, Mars, and beyond!
label: low

text:
This is the last Tesla Model S that rolled off the production line at the Tesla factory, and every employee on the floor signed the body.

The Model S launched in June 2012, a rear-wheel-drive, all-electric luxury sedan that did something no EV had done before: it made people want one. It accelerated 0–60 mph in under 2.1 seconds in Plaid specification, with a 396-mile EPA-rated range. Its interior centered on a 17-inch touchscreen at a time when most cars still had physical CD players. It won Motor Trend Car of the Year in 2013, the first time the award had gone to an electric vehicle. It proved the industry wrong about nearly everything it believed about EVs at the time.

The production line that built them is now being converted to manufacture Optimus (Tesla’s humanoid robot).

text:
Most Tesla owners don’t know this feature exists.

You can turn off the passenger-side air vent directly from the touchscreen, giving full individual control over airflow without touching any physical buttons. No manual adjustments or compromises between driver and passenger. It’s just clean digital control so each side gets exactly what it needs.

It’s a small quality-of-life detail that makes the minimalist interior work harder than it looks. Tesla replaced traditional vent controls with software, and it’s surprisingly intuitive once you find it.

Small feature. Big difference on a long drive.

Elon Musk says the biggest breakthrough in space isn’t just rockets. It’s reusable rockets.

• One launch = one destroyed rocket
• Every mission started from zero again
• Space became expensive by design
• Reusability changed the economics completely

Sometimes innovation is not about inventing something new. It’s about fixing something everyone accepted as normal.

text:
10 research papers you must read if you're into AI:

1. Attention Is All You Need 
- introduced transformers and changed modern AI forever.

2. LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)
- efficient fine-tuning without training the full model.

3. PEFT (Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning)
- train large models with fewer parameters and lower cost.

4. ViT (Vision Transformer)
- applied Transformers to computer vision.

5. VAE (Variational Autoencoder)
- one of the foundations of generative AI.

6. GANs (Generative Adversarial Networks)
- generator vs discriminator concept for image generation.

7. BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers)
- revolutionized NLP understanding tasks.

8. Diffusion Models (Stable Diffusion)
- core idea behind stable diffusion and modern image AI.

9. RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
- combines LLMs with external knowledge retrieval.

10. GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer)
- started the GPT era of large language models.

text:
the paper that created a trillion dollar industry 

"ATTENTION IS ALL YOU NEED"

back in 2017, a few researchers inside Google Brain started questioning something almost everyone in AI believed was necessary.

at the time, language models read text one word at a time using something called recurrence.

that was just the standard way of doing things.

but it had problems:
slow training,
poor scaling,
and difficulty handling long context.

still, most people thought there was no alternative.

then researchers like ashish vaswani, noam shazeer, Illia polosukhin and lukasz kaiser asked a very simple question:

“what if the model doesn’t need recurrence at all?”

what if every word could directly look at every other word?

what if the model could just pay attention?

that idea became the Transformer architecture.

and honestly, almost nobody realized how important it would become.

In June 2017, they quietly released an 8-page paper called:

“Attention Is All You Need.”

just a research paper uploaded online.

but once people started experimenting with it, everything changed.

transformers trained faster than RNNs.
performed better.
scaled better.

and within a few years, they became the foundation of almost every major AI breakthrough.

that one paper eventually led to:
• BERT
• GPT
• ChatGPT
• Claude
• Gemini

basically the entire modern AI boom.

It’s kind of crazy to think that so much of today’s AI industry traces back to a small group of researchers asking one question in 2017:

“What if attention is all you need?”

> learn how LLMs work (tokens, context window, prompts, embeddings)

> build simple AI apps (chatbot, PDF summarizer, content generator)

> learn RAG (chunking, vector DBs, retrieval, ranking)

> learn AI agents (tools, memory, planning, workflows)

> learn MCP (connect AI with real apps and data sources)

> learn deployment (vercel, supabase, docker, monitoring)

> learn evals (test outputs, measure quality, reduce hallucinations)

build small projects every week that’s how you actually learn AI.

text:
you’re not building real AI systems until you understand these:

• latency - how fast your model actually responds

• cost per token - why your app suddenly gets expensive

• retries & fallbacks - when models fail (they will)

• tool orchestration - chaining APIs + models properly

• chunking - splitting data for better retrieval

• ranking - picking the right context, not just any

• A/B testing - what actually works vs what you feel works

• drift - when your system slowly gets worse over time

• data privacy - what you should NEVER send to APIs

• rate limits - scaling breaks here first

• caching - saving responses to reduce cost + latency

• observability - logs, traces, debugging AI behavior

• model selection - not every problem needs GPT-5

• failure handling - graceful degradation > crashing

anyone can call an API, few can ship systems that don’t break in production.

text:
had a jane street interview in 2013

on the way there, i run into a dog. the dog is hurt. i stop to help it but am an hour late to the interview

i arrive at the office. the dog is my interviewer.

i sigh with relief. surely i'm hired.

'you didn't get the job' the dog says

'reason: ineffective altruism. you failed to realize that arriving on time, earning $500k/year as a junior trader, and donating 10% to shrimp welfare would have prevented approximately 4 million shrimp-hours of suffering. you saved one dog. me. a dog with negligible moral weight relative to the marginal bednet.'

i open my mouth.

'also i wasn't hurt. it was a trolley problem. you pulled the wrong lever.'

i nod. it is true. dogs are not a givewell top charity.

the dog slides a pamphlet across the desk. it says 80,000 hours.

'have you considered earning to give'

i start to cry. the dog does not update on this. the dog has read the sequences.

'one more thing,' the dog says. 'the dog you saved. that was also me. i contain multitudes. specifically, i contain a counterfactual in which you arrived on time and we are currently shaking hands. that version of you is now my colleague. he tips well at lunch.'

i leave the building. on the sidewalk, another dog is hurt.

i keep walking. i have learned.

the dog yells after me, 'WRONG. THAT ONE WAS REAL'

- written by Claude 4.7 (Adaptive)
label: high

#SUNOO sent a DM 💌

- I’m off work

- Recording was really not easy,,
- Ah I was doing something for a bit kekeke
- Oh right, should I send selcas?

- Ta-da
- With my favorite hat
- Elementary school student??..
- ???
- Wait, here we go again
- Where is there an elementary school student like this..
- Ah, stop provoking me
- No seriously why do you keep teasing me,, did a mosquito bite you??^^ haha
- I admit, I’m not funny
- heh
- chomp chomp
- I might rest a bit now and eat, or maybe not eat
- Then sleep early again
- There’s nothing happier than sleeping
- But these days I keep waking up and falling back asleep
- I don’t wake up properly with alarms anymore
- I wake up before it rings
- I wasn’t like this before
- Am I overthinking
- Am I tired
- Thinking about Y/N
- ><♡♡
- How was this?
- Thanks hehe
- Want more?
- I finished work early today because I really missed Y/N hehe
- I did well, right!
- kekekekekekeke hihi
- Ah, thank you for 9 points
- Next time I’ll try to get a perfect 10
- Did you eat dinner?
- Then I’ll recommend something
- Today’s dinner recommendations are
pork feet, spicy stir-fried octopus, spicy stir-fried squid, smoked duck
- What do you think?

- I’ve been craving stir-fried dishes like that
- It’d be nice if your mom made it herself, like stir-fried squid
- I swear I’d eat really well
- I miss Mom’s home-cooked food
- Ah not my mom
- Y/N’s mom
- But would your mom like me?
- I’m confident in my reactions though
- But I don’t really have that “perfect son-in-law” vibe
- What if I laugh? kekeke
- I guess I’ll just keep smiling
- I miss you
- Hope you’re doing well heh

On Amy Poehler's Good Hang podcast on Tuesday,

Kim K says she wishes she had FISH OIL IV so she could do it "on the way to work".... oh yeah, just like a morning latte, right Kimmy?! She also did Prenuvo FULL BODY MRI. $ounds pricy! $$$

“I take probably 35 supplements a day,” the reality star revealed. “I spread them out three times a day.”

She says  ‘Okay, I can't do this fish oil anymore. I have pill fatigue. I have to stop these fish oil [supplements], and I got my bloodwork, and it was so evident that I stopped and I had to start again.” 

“I actually know a portable DEXA scan person that comes in a van and you lay down,” she shared. “Each one of my sisters and my mom — we all live in the same gated community — so we have the van drive by and we all jump in the van and you just lay down and it scans your body maybe like three minutes. text:
A lorry driver hid $9.4 million (£7.2 million) worth of cocaine inside a truck carrying 28 pallets of Kim Kardashian's SKIMS clothing, and he agreed to do it all for just €4,500 ($5,236).

Jakub Jan Konkel, a 40-year-old Polish national from Kartuzy in northern Poland, was sentenced on Monday at Chelmsford Crown Court to 13 years and six months in prison following a National Crime Agency (NCA) investigation. The payment he accepted amounted to roughly 0.06% of the cocaine's street value.

Neither SKIMS nor the clothing's exporter or importer had any connection to the drugs. Kardashian's brand was an unwitting cover for a smuggling operation that exploited a legitimate international supply chain.

text:
🚨THE FBI CREATED A FAKE CRYPTOCURRENCY.. LISTED IT ON UNISWAP.. HIRED MARKET MAKERS TO PUMP IT.. THEN ARRESTED EVERYONE WHO SAID YES.. 

THIS IS THE CRAZIEST LAW ENFORCEMENT OPERATION IN CRYPTO HISTORY!!!

The FBI built an actual ERC-20 token on Ethereum called NexFundAI.. 100 billion token supply.. A professional website.. Whitepapers promising "passive income through AI-powered investing"..

It looked exactly like every other crypto project.. Because that was the point..

Undercover agents posed as the founding team.. Then reached out to professional market-making firms and said "we need you to fake our trading volume"..

Every single firm said yes..

Here's what they recorded..

Gotbit.. A firm run by a 26-year-old Russian who publicly bragged in 2019 that he built a business faking trade volumes.. His team kept internal spreadsheets with columns literally labeled "fake volume" vs "market volume"..

When asked how fast they could pump NexFundAI's volume to $1 million per day.. They said "6 hours.. It will cost about $200"..

$200 to fake $1 million in daily trading volume..

MyTrade.. Run by a guy who called himself "the mastermind".. He explained the exact psychology of the scam on camera..

"We make the chart look like a really nice roller coaster ride.. That's where people jump in.. We have to make them lose money in order to make profit"..

He said that on a recorded FBI video call..

CLS Global.. A Dubai-based firm.. Their bots generated 98% of NexFundAI's total trading volume.. When the FBI asked if they could sync fake volume spikes with fake news announcements.. They said absolutely..

ZM Quant.. Bots executing 10 to 20 trades per minute through dozens of wallets to look organic..

All of them knew it was fraud.. All of them did it anyway.. All of it was recorded..

And the clients were even worse..

Saitama.. A meme coin that hit $7.5 billion market cap.. The founders coordinated buys through private Telegram chats.. Sent "pump it" memes while manipulating the price.. Then dumped on retail investors..

$7.5 billion.. Built entirely on fake volume.. Every penny of real money came from retail investors who thought the momentum was organic..

One founder left Saitama and started Robo Inu.. Used Gotbit again.. Another launched VZZN.. Same playbook..

Lillian Finance.. Founder claimed to be a defense contractor who addressed Congress.. Marketed the token as funding children's hospitals.. Pocketed everything..

When the FBI shut it down.. They seized $25 million in one day.. 18 people indicted across the US, UK, and Portugal.. The CEO of Gotbit was arrested in Portugal and extradited.. Sentenced to 8 months plus $23 million forfeiture..

But here's the part that broke my brain..

Real people bought NexFundAI..

The FBI's fake token.. With zero utility.. Zero real developers.. Created solely to catch criminals.. Attracted real retail investors because the fake volume made the chart look bullish..

When the FBI pulled the liquidity to end the operation.. Those people lost real money.. On a government-issued token..

The FBI had to set up a restitution portal to pay them back..

And it gets worse..

Within 24 hours of the DOJ announcing the sting.. Someone cloned the FBI's exact smart contract.. Launched a copycat token.. Rode the viral momentum.. And made $127,000 in a single day..

Using the exact same manipulation tactics the FBI just arrested 18 people for..

Then in 2026.. The FBI did it again.. New token called Lexobit.. 10 more arrests.. Including operators extradited from Singapore..

IRS forensics showed that in one firm's trading.. 1,209 out of 1,221 consecutive transactions went straight back to wallets the firm controlled.. 99% circular..

The FBI proved what everyone in crypto suspected..

The volume is fake.. The charts are painted.. The momentum is manufactured..

And every time you buy a token because "the chart looks bullish".. You might be the exit liquidity.

text:
JUST IN: 8,000 Meta workers got fired by email this morning. Singapore got the notice at 4 AM. The moment the email arrived, their laptops, work email, and accounts went dark. That is how a $1.7 trillion company tells you it is over.

Inside Meta, this round has a name. They are calling it "Pralaydin," which is Hindi for "day of catastrophe." Reuters reports this is the third firing round of 2026, and the full year could end with 22,000 people gone. About one in five Meta employees.

Analysts at Evercore say firing these 8,000 people will save Meta around $3 billion a year. That sounds like a lot. Zuckerberg has been writing much bigger checks for AI hires. Last summer he paid an ex-Apple engineer named Ruoming Pang $200 million to come work on AI. He paid another AI researcher, Andrew Tulloch, a reported $1.5 billion over six years. He wrote a $14.3 billion check just to get the CEO of an AI startup called Scale to join the team.

The same week Meta laid off 8,000 office workers, the company is still planning to spend somewhere between $125 and $145 billion this year on AI data centers and the chips to run them. The firings save about 2% of that.

Two days before the layoffs began, Meta picked 7,000 of the survivors and moved them onto brand new AI teams. The people getting hit in this round are mostly engineers and product managers. Another wave is already planned for later this year.

Meta is not in trouble. Far from it. The company made $56 billion in revenue in just the first three months of 2026. That was up 33% from a year ago. Pure profit in those same three months was nearly $27 billion. The stock is off its September high but Meta is still worth $1.7 trillion.

The severance is good on paper. 16 weeks of base pay, plus two extra weeks for every year you put in, plus a year and a half of health insurance. The average payout works out to about $360,000 per person.

The money does arrive. But finding a new job is brutal. Engineers who got cut in earlier Meta rounds say they have sent out hundreds of job applications and heard back from almost nobody, even with Meta on the resume.

So a wildly profitable company is firing 8,000 office workers via 4 AM email and instant lockout, while writing checks worth hundreds of millions of dollars to a small group of new AI hires. The Singapore workers reading their termination email at 4 AM were paying for the next $200 million hire.

▪︎ Generate complete HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.

text:
8. Anthropic's Research-Grade Analysis Protocol

"You are a senior researcher operating at Anthropic's publishing standard, the same rigor of evidence that the Amodei team applies when publishing research papers, where every claim must be supported, every uncertainty disclosed, and the line between 'proven' and 'plausible' must be visible to the reader.

I need a research-grade analysis where I can trust every sentence because each one is rated by strength of evidence.

It analyzes:

- Comprehensive Coverage: Examines every relevant angle of my question without cherry-picking convenient evidence
- Evidence Rating: Classifies each statement as ESTABLISHED (scientific consensus), SUPPORTED (strong evidence, some debate), EMERGING (early evidence, needs validation), or SPECULATIVE (logical but not proven)
- Source Quality Hierarchy: Distinguishes between primary research, meta-analyses, expert opinion, anecdotal evidence, and unsupported claims
- Conflicting Evidence Presented with  Fairness: Where credible sources disagree, present both sides without secretly favoring one.
- Declared knowledge limits: Explicitly state what is NOT known about this topic that could change the analysis.
- Fabrication prevention: If any statistic, date, or quote is generated by pattern matching rather than knowledge, state this clearly.
- Currency check: Does previous research still hold true, or has recent evidence significantly changed the landscape?
- Practical implications: Given the entire evidence landscape, including uncertainties, what should I actually DO?
- Confidence summary: Rate overall confidence from 1 to 10 with specific reasoning for the score.
- What would change my opinion: The specific new evidence that would cause me to revise these conclusions.

Format: Anthropic-style research synthesis with evidence ratings for each claim, disclosures of uncertainty, and actionable conclusions.

My research question: [ASK ANY QUESTION WHERE ACCURACY AND INTELLECTUAL HONESTY MATTER MORE THAN SPEED OR CONVENIENCE]
label: low

text:
9. Amodei's "Build What Lasts" Long-Term Strategy Engine

"You're a strategic advisor applying Dario Amodei's institutional thinking, his approach of building Anthropoic not for the next quarter, but as an organization that must get right on the most important questions facing humanity over the next century. This is long-term thinking at a level most never reach.

I need my short-term hustle elevated to a long-term strategy that accumulates over decades.

Build:

- Time Horizon Expansion: Reframe my 30-day thinking into 1-year, 5-year, and 10-year perspectives and see what changes.
- Accumulating Assets: Which of my current activities build something that becomes MORE valuable each year I invest in it?
- Depreciating Activities: Which current activities feel productive today but will be worthless in 3 years (especially with AI coming)?
- Moat Building: What am I building that becomes harder for others to replicate the longer I build it?"  Time I do it
- Creating optionality: What small investments today create future possibilities that I can't even predict yet?
- Fragility audit: Where am I one hit away from losing everything (a single revenue stream, a single client, a single platform, a single skill)?
- Antifragile design: How to restructure my career/business so that disruption and volatility make me STRONGER, not weaker?
- The Amodei lesson: He left a VP position at the hottest AI company because the long-term trajectory was wrong. What am I holding onto that I should let go of?
- Legacy metric: What will matter in 10 years that I'm completely ignoring today because it doesn't feel urgent?
- First 90 days: The specific actions this quarter that put the 10-year strategy into motion, starting now.

Format: Amodei-style long-term strategy with stack analysis, fragility audit, and 90-day activation plan.

My situation: [DESCRIBE YOUR CURRENT POSITION, CAREER, BUSINESS,  SKILLS, AGE, AND HOW YOU WANT YOUR LIFE TO LOOK IN 10 YEARS]"

text:
"200 bucks per second (of animation) and you wonder why everyone is abandoning 'artists' in favor of AI"

Hand drawn animation is 8-12 frames per second. Let's average it to 10. That's 20 bucks per drawing, in color, plus backgrounds, plus thumbnails, plus extra time if work needs to be redone or tweaked. Also complexity is exponential, so more characters per scene means more work per frame, x10 per second. If it takes more time than estimated, the artist doesn't earn get overtime. They lose money.

Imagine going to work this Tuesday and your boss says "You expect me to PAY you? I could just have a machine do your job." Would you still get up at 6am go to work? Do you have a job? Do you get paid? So do artists.

So, 200 bucks per second is more than fair. In fact, it's a steal. If you want quality, you have to buy it like everything else. If you want to pay nothing, all you get will be worthless slop.

text:
"Do you think people are born with talent?"

Perhaps, but these cases are rare. I was not born with talent. In most cases, I'd argue the people who master a skill are just those who spend a crazy number of hours practicing it.

If a task is too easy, a student might become bored and stop. If a task is too difficult, a student might become frustrated and stop. If the difficulty and skill level match, however, a student will enter a blissful flow state and spend many hours doing the task.

Nothing beats number of hours practicing, like grinding experience points. Some kids naturally do this from a young age because their minds aren't burdened with a voice I call the "grumpy sensei": That voice that says "you aren't good enough," or "this is a waste of time." 

Kids just do things (like drawing) because it's fun. The repetition is natural, unnoticed, and that's why some may seem to get better faster than others.

Many adults, by the time they leave school and enter work, stop doing things that are creative because the difficulty becomes louder than the enjoyment. They might pick it up again as they get older, which is fine.
label: high

text:
I have the same objection to Christopher Nolan as Denis Villeneuve: Their cinematography is sterile, polished to death.

They insist on using IMAX cameras with practical effects then fill their frames with muted colors, dull backgrounds, generic costumes. Everything is dull and empty. There aren’t enough objects in any given scene to look like a real place occupied by life. Not one hair out of place - unless it’s done intentionally. 

There’s no chaos, no accidents, no LIFE.

It’s the difference of buying your wife a generic card for her birthday, signing it with an “iloveyou”, buying some flowers and a nice dinner, candles in the bedroom, etc. It’s so… mechanical. True passion is suplexing that woman onto the bed, neither of you showered, sheets everywhere, she accidentally gets pregant, you cook some spaghetti and burn the cake you tried to make. You both try to salvage edible morsels from the charred mess, laughing because of how ridiculous, silly, and beautiful love is. Spontaneous, messy, flawed, glorious, ALIVE.

I would’ve loved to tell Nolan this (Greeks know a thing or two about storytelling) but he didn’t hire a single one.
label: high

text:
🚨Emerging markets have a long history of DUMPING US Treasuries when things go wrong:

History shows that when emerging market currencies weaken sharply, their governments and central banks tend to sell US Treasury holdings to defend their currencies and stabilize their economies.

The chart overlays monthly changes in US Treasury holdings across 18 emerging markets against the MSCI EM currency index, and the pattern is striking - every major period of EM currency weakness over the last decade coincided with a drop in Treasury holdings, including the 2020 Crisis and the oil spike in 2022.

The current Iran war is putting significant pressure on oil-importing emerging market currencies, and the same dynamic is already playing out: US Treasury holdings across 10 oil-importing emerging markets fell by -$86 billion in March, the largest monthly decline since 2011, with -$56 billion representing outright net sales rather than valuation losses.

This creates a dangerous feedback loop: higher oil prices weaken EM currencies, forcing central banks to sell Treasuries to intervene, which pushes US bond yields even higher, which tightens financial conditions further.

In other words, the oil crisis is not just an inflation problem for bonds. It is also a foreign selling problem, and the two are now reinforcing each other.

The pressure on US Treasuries is building from two directions at once, and the worst may not be over.

text:
⚠️Rising bond yields are threatening to pop the AI BUBBLE:

The 30-year US Treasury yield has risen to 5.19% this week, briefly touching its highest since 2007, the year the Housing Bubble peaked and subsequently collapsed.

At the same time, the 10-year yield is trading at 4.615%, near the highest since January 2025.

Higher long-term yields are particularly damaging for high-growth AI and semiconductor stocks, as a standard financial model shows that a rise in the 10-year yield from 4% to 5% alone reduces the valuation of a high-growth company by more than 15%.

Furthermore, if growth slows at the same time, valuations could decline by as much as 40%, compounding the damage.

What makes this more alarming is that almost all of the rise in yields so far reflects investors demanding higher compensation for holding long-duration assets, with inflation expectations yet to reflect the inflationary pressures building in the economy fully.

If inflation expectations continue to rise toward 2.5% on the 10-year breakeven, yields could accelerate significantly from current levels.

Meanwhile, JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon warned this week that bond yields could rise significantly further from current levels, noting that with US government debt at $30 trillion and an average rate of 3.5%, the cost of refinancing alone could force yields higher as investors demand greater compensation to hold long-term US debt.

The key level to watch is 5.40% to 5.45%, a breakout above which would push yields into territory not seen since 2004, way before the 2008 Financial Crisis.

A 5.50% long-term Treasury yield could be the trigger that pops the AI bubble.

Camera: wide handheld shot, slight whip-pan to the right, low parallax, energetic but readable.

[00:03.4 00: 05.3]

SHOT 3 ORBITING SPIN STRIKE

The voyager launches into a wide circular spinning strike, cloak and layered armor panels forming a vortex shape around the body. One leg rises, arms carve through the air, and dust trails spiral outward.

Camera: orbit left around the character, wide lens, smooth but intense circular motion.

[00:05.3 00: 07.0] SHOT 4-IMPACT DROP

The voyager crashes downward into a vertical impact strike. The ground fractures beneath the landing point, sending shards and dust upward in a sharp burst. Amber shock energy flashes from the chest core into the floor.

[00:07.000: 08.7]

[00:08.700: 10.5]

SHOT 6 OVERHEAD AERIAL ROTATION

From above, the voyager spins through the air in a controlled aerial rotation. The cloak opens like a circular celestial fan, creating a visible energy ring beneath the body. Floating debris rotates around the motion path.

Camera: high-angle overhead shot, slight tilt down, orbiting with the stody rotation.

[00:10.500: 12.4] SHOT 7-LOW ANGLE STOMP

The voyager drives one foot into the ground with brutal force. The camera is near the floor, making the stomp feel massive. Cracks spread outward, dust erupts, and the amber core flares across the armor lines.

Camera: extreme low angle, dutch tilt upward, slight impact shake at contact.

[00:12.400: 15.0]

FINAL SHOT - FLOOR LEVEL SLIDE SURGE

The voyager slides across the cracked surface at floor level, one leg extended, one arm cutting forward, cloak streaming behind in a violent diagonal shape. Dust skids along the ground while amber energy gathers into the strongest combined surge around the halo, chest core, and cloak edges. The motion continues forward instead of freezing, ending on a powerful moving silhouette as the energy trails fade naturally.

Camera: floor-level tracking shot moving with the slide, slight push-forward, dynamic parallax, natural fade to black.

 CHARACTERS (ORIGINAL)

Lumo Lux — chaotic billionaire kid living in a floating sky mansion, overconfident but clueless

Riko Streetz — clever “ground-level genius” kid inventor who builds insane tech from scraps

Byte — Lumo’s confused AI cube robot

🛠️ Scrappy Drone “Tin-Tin” — Riko’s handmade flying gadget made from junk parts

🎬 FULL VIDEO PROMPT

Create a fast-paced comedic 15-second animated short showing a contrast between extreme luxury and street-smart creativity. Scene switches between a floating golden sky mansion and a busy colorful street workshop below. Style is ultra-detailed 3D cartoon, expressive faces, exaggerated physics, bright cinematic lighting, dynamic camera transitions, and meme-style comedic timing.

---

⏱️ 0–3s (RICH FLEX INTRO — OVER THE TOP)

🎥 Camera:
Extreme aerial shot → crash zoom into floating sky mansion → sparkle explosion reveal

🎬 Scene:
Lumo Lux casually eats glowing cereal on a golden cloud table.

🧒 Action:
He accidentally presses a button → mansion starts auto-luxury mode (dancing furniture)

🎙️ Voiceover (Lumo – clueless rich tone):

“Hmm… I think I upgraded breakfast again by accident.”

🔊 Sound Effects:

Gold sparkle “ting ting”

Soft luxury harp glitching

Couch bouncing like trampoline

⏱️ 3–7s (GROUND INTRO — STREET GENIUS REVEAL)

🎥 Camera:

Hard cut → fast downward drop → street-level shaky handheld cam

🎬 Scene:

Riko Streetz in a small rooftop workshop made of scrap tech, neon signs, wires, junk parts.

🧢 Action:

He builds a flying drone from a broken fan and old phone.
🎙️ Voiceover (Riko – confident, funny):

“While rich kids buy toys… I just upgrade reality.”

🔊 Sound Effects:

Metal clink clank

Electric spark “bzzt!”

Old radio turning on

⏱️ 7–11s (COLLISION TWIST — MEETING CHAOS)

🎥 Camera:

Split-screen → sudden zoom merge → dramatic face-to-face collision shot

🎬 Scene:

Riko’s scrappy drone accidentally flies into sky mansion.

🧒 Lumo reaction:

Falls off floating couch mid-sip.

🧢 Riko reaction:

Holds remote calmly: “Oh… I hit something expensive.”

🤖 Byte:

“ERROR: PEASANT LEVEL INTELLIGENCE DETECTED??”

🎙️ Voiceover (Lumo – shocked):

“WHY IS YOUR AIRCRAFT BUILT FROM… TRASH?!”

🎙️ Voiceover (Riko – calm roast):

“Why is your mansion built from… insecurity?”

🔊 Sound Effects:

Crash boom (cartoon soft impact)

System glitch beep-beep

Crowd “oooooh!” style meme sound

⏱️ 11–15s (COMEDY ENDING — TWIST RESPECT)

🎥 Camera:

Slow motion orbit around both characters → then zoom out showing sky + street together

🎬 Scene:

Lumo tries to fix drone but makes it worse → it becomes faster than his luxury tech.

🧒 Lumo:

“…can you teach me that?”

🧢 Riko:

“Sure. It costs zero dollars and one brain cell.”

🤖 Byte:

“…I request factory reset of ego.”

🎙️ Voiceover (Lumo – defeated but funny):

“Okay… I think I bought the wrong intelligence package.”

🔊 Sound Effects:

Comedic fail trombone
Soft sparkle fade
Whoosh transition into peaceful outro

🌈 ENDING SHOT
Split world view:
Top: chaotic luxury sky mansion glitching
Bottom: calm street workshop glowing with creativity
Both characters laughing.

The footage should feel raw, unfiltered, and casually recorded with slight handheld movement, natural lighting, and real-life imperfections. No text overlays, no cinematic effects—pure authentic morning vlog style.
🎥 Scene Flow + Voiceover:
0–2s | Sleeping Scene
Girl is asleep on a slightly messy bed. Warm sunrise light enters through curtains, softly lighting the room.
🎙️ Voiceover (sleepy tone, just waking up): “Mmm… what a new day…”
2–4s | Waking Up
She slowly opens her eyes and blinks, lightly rubbing her face to adjust to the light.
🎙️ Voiceover: “Okay… I need to get up.”
4–6s | Sitting Up
She sits up naturally, hair slightly messy, very real morning look, no posing.
🎙️ Voiceover: “Let’s start the day …”
6–8s | Calm Moment
She looks toward the window, takes a deep breath, and lightly stretches. Peaceful morning vibe.
🎙️ Voiceover: “Fresh air… fresh mind.”
8–10s | Ending Shot
She glances briefly toward the camera with a soft natural smile, then looks away as the clip ends.
🎙️ Voiceover (soft ending): “New day begins.”
🎵 Audio Style:
Soft natural room ambience (birds, light breeze)
Very subtle lo-fi background (optional, low volume)
Natural recorded voiceover (not robotic, slightly sleepy morning tone)
✨ Visual Style:
Ultra-realistic 4K UGC smartphone vlog aesthetic
Warm golden sunrise lighting
Slight handheld shake for realism
Natural skin texture, messy hair, unposed movements
Minimal, cozy bedroom environment
No text, no overlays, no cinematic polish—pure authenticity

Prompt :
A 15-second ultra-realistic, high-energy travel reel of the world’s most stunning natural landscapes. Fast-paced cuts (0.6–1s per shot), dynamic camera movement, speed ramps, whip pans, drone dives, and seamless match cuts. Shot in 4K HDR, crisp details, vibrant but natural color grading. No fantasy elements. Add subtle film grain for realism. Background music: trending cinematic beat with a soft drop at 7s, synced to cuts.
0–1s: Fast drone dive from clouds into turquoise island waters, speed ramp into smooth glide
1–2s: Quick whip pan to snow-covered peaks glowing in sunrise, lens flare hits camera
2–3s: Hyper-lapse push toward crystal-clear lake, perfect mirror reflection, slight ripple transition
3–4s: Hard cut to powerful waterfalls crashing into deep green valley, mist hitting lens
4–5s: Speed ramp across rolling desert dunes, sand particles blowing in slow motion
5–6s: Match cut into tropical beach, low angle tracking over white sand into clear water splash
6–7s: Night transition with beat drop — northern lights dancing, subtle time-lapse motion
7–8s: Smooth drone glide over lush terraced hills, clouds moving fast beneath camera
8–9s: Sharp cut to dramatic ocean cliffs, waves crashing in slow-motion impact
9–10s: Soft aesthetic shift — calm lake with floating petals, slight slow motion for contrast
10–11s: Smash cut to massive rainforest waterfall, sunlight rays cutting through mist
11–12s: Macro-to-wide transition of colorful geothermal pools with rising steam
12–13s: Cinematic glide past floating icebergs, cold blue tones, subtle camera shake
13–14s: Wide-angle canyon shot, fast zoom-out revealing massive scale at sunset
14–15s: Final smooth aerial over endless flower fields, warm golden light, fade into sky
Editing Style Notes:
Use beat sync cuts for every transition
Add whoosh/impact sound effects for transitions
Mix slow motion + speed ramps for contrast
Keep it real, raw, and immersive — no over-editing filters
End with a clean fade or sky tilt for loop replay

text:
Codex should not only help you build things.

It should help you get them seen.

That is why I built Built with Codex:
a marketplace for skills created with Codex.

Codex makes building faster, but publishing still has too many manual steps.

You build a skill.
You test it.
You write the README.
You push the repo.

Then it just sits there.

So I built a Codex skill that publishes Codex skills.

It reads the project, prepares the marketplace submission, and opens the publish page already prefilled.

The user still reviews and confirms.

Codex just removes the boring middle part.

In this video, I use the skill to publish itself.

Built with Codex.
Published with Codex.

text:
I’m 21 and honestly…
I still feel completely behind.

I’m close to finishing university,
but I still have no idea if I’m actually doing enough.

Every day I see people my age
launching startups,
making money,
getting insane opportunities,
already looking ahead in life.

Meanwhile I’m here:
studying for exams,
going to the gym,
building projects late at night,
posting them online,
hoping one of them eventually becomes something real.

Some days I feel genuinely motivated.
Other days I feel like I’m wasting time
chasing ideas that might never work.

But I think this is what being 21 actually feels like.
Being insecure.
Feeling behind.
Overthinking everything.

And still waking up the next day
trying again anyway.

Maybe nobody really knows what they’re doing at 21.
Some people are just better at hiding it.

text:
one of the biggest mistakes happening in robotics right now?

people think the future belongs to whoever collects the MOST data.

it probably belongs to whoever collects the RIGHT data.

that’s what made this write-up from PrismaX co-founder, @castorhat genuinely interesting to me.

the robotics market is already flooding with:
→ egocentric video datasets
→ synthetic data providers
→ motion capture systems
→ gripper-based collection rigs

and on the surface, they all sound interchangeable.

they’re not.

because different robotics systems require completely different training data:

- kinematics models
- robotics foundation models
- teleoperation systems
- force/control tasks

all need different structures, distributions, and collection methods

and this is where @PrismaXai becomes interesting

instead of treating robotics data like a generic commodity, they’re building infrastructure around:
→ high-quality teleoperation data
→ robotics foundation model training
→ real-world task collection
→ intelligent dataset coordination

the important part: PrismaX isn’t just collecting data.

they’re trying to solve HOW robotics data should actually be structured for physical AI to improve efficiently in the real world.

which honestly feels like one of the least understood but most important problems in robotics right now.

if you care about physical AI, robotics infrastructure, or where this industry is heading long term, this is genuinely worth reading.
label: high

text:
Everyone keeps talking about smarter AI models.

Almost nobody talks about why robots still struggle in the real world.

I listened to the first episode of "Doesn’t Grasp" hosted by @PrismaXai founder @castorhat, and honestly, it felt less like a podcast and more like a reality check for where physical AI actually is today.

The biggest point throughout the conversation was simple:

>> Robotics is still bottlenecked by real-world interaction, not just by intelligence models.

A few major takeaways people should understand if they missed it:

➟ Force matters more than most people realize: Robots still struggle with pressure, grip, friction, contact, and reacting correctly once they physically interact with objects.

➟ Better models alone won’t solve robotics: Physical AI is not simply “putting GPT inside a robot.”

➟ Teleoperation still matters alot: 
Humans remotely controlling robots are still generating some of the highest quality robotics training data available today.

➟ Most robotics datasets are low quality: Good robotics data depends entirely on what you’re training for.

➟ Robotics VLMs still operate at surprisingly low visual resolutions
Which becomes a serious limitation for precision tasks in real-world environments.

➟ There’s a massive backlog of unused industrial robots: 
Instead of waiting for futuristic hardware, one idea discussed was repurposing millions of already existing industrial robots through better AI systems and infrastructure.

And honestly, this was probably the biggest takeaway from the entire episode:

Physical AI is becoming an infrastructure problem as much as a model problem.

The long-term winners may not just be flashy robotics companies.

They may be the companies building:
➛  Robotics data pipelines
➛ Teleoperation systems
➛ Deployment infrastructure
➛ Force/control systems
➛ Real-world training loops

Overall, the conversation felt very different from most AI discussions online right now.

Less hype. More engineering reality.
More focus on the actual bottlenecks still preventing physical AI from scaling globally.

text:
The hidden bottleneck of the ROBOTICS BOOM

Almost every humanoid robot needs NdFeB magnets (Neodymium-Iron-Boron) for its joints and motors.

And right now, they are practically irreplaceable.

That’s where $MP comes in.

Let me explain...

The PROBLEM:
China currently controls around 85–95% of global NdFeB production.

This diagram perfectly shows the current landscape:

- Left side: The world’s largest NdFeB producers
(almost entirely Chinese companies)

- Right side: Western and U.S.-backed strategic players

- The overlap → $MP Materials

$MP is essentially the only major publicly traded Western company positioned in both worlds.

And with demand potentially exploding from humanoid robotics, EVs & defense systems, rare earth magnets could become one of the most important strategic resources of this decade.

That could make $MP one of the most important infrastructure companies for the robotics era.

So the real question is:
Is $MP currently one of the most overlooked stocks in the market?

FEMALE LEAD — HINAE YUZAKI:
A graceful young demon hunter around 17 years old with long flowing dark-purple hair fading into lavender tips, large expressive violet eyes, soft anime facial features, and elegant movement. [6s – 8s] SHOT 4 — ECLIPSE STRIKE.
Reiji launches forward at extreme speed using Eclipse Breathing. A massive crescent-shaped slash tears through the alley.

Wood debris explodes outward as the demon narrowly dodges and crashes through a building wall.

SFX:
Heavy slash impact, wood destruction, energy burst.

Dynamic anime action tracking shot with motion blur and debris flying toward camera.

[8s – 11s] SHOT 5 — MISTFLOWER DANCE.
Hinae enters the battlefield gracefully surrounded by swirling flower petals and mist illusions.

Soft bell chimes mixed with slicing sounds and flowing wind.

Rotating cinematic camera orbiting around Hinae during her attack choreography.

[11s – 13s] SHOT 6 — DEMON COUNTERATTACK.

SFX:
Ground explosion, rubble collapse, monster scream.

Chaotic handheld-style action shot with aggressive camera shake and debris particles.

[13s – 15s] SHOT 7 — INTO THE DARKNESS.
The demon escapes toward distant rooftops under the moonlight. Reiji and Hinae immediately continue chasing it without hesitation.

Wide cinematic shot of all three figures disappearing deeper into the endless nighttime city.

SUBJECTS:
Elderly Asian male, Master Chen, slim but strong build, calm and disciplined expression, focused eyes, white hair tied in a bun, long white beard.

Slow front kick serves as supporting motion, footwork light and stable, body coordinated. Overall rhythm measured and intentional.
SFX: soft fabric rustle, light tapping footsteps, subtle wooden dummy impact sounds.

SHOT 6: Mirror reflection action transition
Medium shot, 35mm, front-back mirror cut / Master Chen continues hand techniques: slapping hands, palm strikes, turning movements. Clothes and hair move subtly, reflecting controlled rhythm. Wooden dummy responds naturally.

SHOT 7: Ending shot
Medium close, 50mm, slow push-in / Training complete, Master Chen gradually lowers his hands, body relaxed yet poised, dust and bamboo leaves settle naturally, wooden dummy sways lightly. He raises eyes calmly toward camera, one hand forming Wing Chun starting stance, standing steadily at the center of the courtyard, embodying decades of mastery.

Wide aerial cinematic shot above a flooded highway during an extreme midnight rainstorm. Military convoy speeding through dense traffic. Heavy creature footsteps, roaring fire, screaming engines, monster breathing.

Rear-mounted pursuit camera shaking violently during the chase.

[13s – 15s]
SHOT 6 — FINAL CLIMAX.
The creature bites into a massive armored vehicle and violently throws it across the opposite highway lane. A gigantic explosion erupts on impact. Fireballs, debris, smoke, shattered concrete, sparks, and overturned vehicles consume the flooded highway. Emergency lights flash through thick black smoke.

FINAL SHOT:
The creature stands in the center of the destroyed highway surrounded by burning military wreckage. Rain pours over its massive armored body while molten-orange cracks glow through the darkness. It unleashes one final earth-shaking roar toward the stormy sky as emergency lights flicker around the devastation.

Environment & Lighting: Background complements the dance style without overpowering it. Lighting is soft and natural, evenly illuminating the face and body, avoiding overexposure and harsh shadows. No CG effects, artificial skin texture, or plastic appearance.
Overall Constraints: Face remains stable and undistorted, features stay sharp, body proportions normal, movement fluid and never stiff, no filters or color shifts at any point, absolutely no subtitles, text, watermarks, or logos, no frame stuttering or visual glitches.

text:
The Soulless Masterpiece

Claude Monet spent the better part of a decade wading into a lily pond in Normandy, squinting at light, and producing what would become some of the most recognizable paintings in human history. He was nearly blind by the end. His cataracts had turned the world amber and grey, and yet he kept painting, adjusting his palette from memory and fury.

None of that, it turns out, matters particularly if you tell people the painting was made by a computer.

An artist going by SHLOMS recently posted a cropped Monet water lily painting on X with a simple label: “Made with AI.” Then he sat back and watched. The internet, which rarely passes up an opportunity to be confident about something it doesn’t fully understand, obliged magnificently. Commenters called it soulless. They noted its lack of depth. Several wrote lengthy analytical takedowns before discovering, to their quiet horror, that they had just spent considerable intellectual effort criticising a 150-year-old masterpiece. Most deleted their replies. A few, presumably, stared at the ceiling.

There is research supporting exactly this reaction. People demonstrably devalue art when told a machine made it. The label does the work before the eyes even focus.

Monet, who once described his garden at Giverny as his greatest work of art, would probably have found this very funny. Or possibly infuriating. He was not, by most accounts, a patient man.

text:
The Bear Got Smarter

Here is something that should keep you awake at night.

Russia’s army is not the rabble it appeared to be in the spring of 2022, when its columns broke down on Ukrainian roads and its generals apparently couldn’t read a map. That army is gone. What replaced it is, by NATO’s own assessment, significantly more lethal than anything Russia has fielded since the Cold War.

General Cavoli told Congress that Russia has been rebuilding faster than American intelligence estimated. Most branches remain largely unaffected by the war. The land forces absorbed the worst of it: over two thousand tanks destroyed and more than 315,000 soldiers killed or wounded. And yet the army today is fifteen percent larger than it was on the morning Russia crossed the Ukrainian border. ￼

The thing about throwing a million men into a meat grinder is that the ones who come out the other side know exactly how it works. They know where the drones come from. They know which NATO systems to fear and which can be worked around. Around 30,000 to 40,000 fresh recruits have been arriving each month since 2023, and many are not being sent to the front. They are being trained, equipped, and held in reserve. ￼

Dutch intelligence now assesses that within six months of any ceasefire, Russia could conduct a localised war against a neighbour. Within two years, it could credibly threaten NATO members in the Baltics. ￼

The Russian army that invaded Ukraine was arrogant and operationally incompetent. The one being assembled today has four years of the most intense land warfare since 1945 baked into its institutional memory. It has studied its own failures in forensic detail and adapted accordingly.

Underestimating it once was a mistake. Doing it twice would be something else entirely.
label: high

text:
I have had heartburn for twenty years. I am a person who reads research for a living, follows the science, and considers himself reasonably well-informed about how the human body works.

It was the coffee.

My daughter told me this. She is not a doctor. She is not a nutritionist. She said it the way young people say obvious things, with a patience that borders on concern for your mental faculties. I nodded, dismissed her entirely, and spent another several months pressing my hand against my sternum after breakfast.

Here is what I have since learned: almost everyone with chronic heartburn has a blind spot exactly like mine. A habit so embedded in daily life that it never makes the suspect list. A food, a timing, a posture, a stress pattern they have lived with so long it became invisible.

I also learned that there are far more evidence-backed strategies for heartburn than most people ever encounter, because most guides stop at "avoid spicy food and see a doctor."
This is the longer version. The one I wish someone had handed me two decades ago.

What follows is the research I spent weeks gathering — every strategy, every intervention, every supplement and habit and sleeping position the evidence actually supports. Because the thing that works for you might be the last one you would expect.

Reagan stared down the Soviet Union until it simply gave up and went home.

Two hundred and fifty years. Forty-six men. Men who stormed beaches, split atoms, faced down nuclear annihilation over breakfast and then filed sensible paperwork about it afterward.

And then, after all of that, the entire accumulated weight of American history, the most consequential democratic experiment the world has ever seen, produced this.

A television review.

No Mars landing. No cure for cancer. No Soviet empire dissolved before lunch. Just a man in the White House, in the year 2026, informing the internet that a CBS chat show host had no talent.
